figures.caption: Figure 6 | Per-problem binning procedure. For each problem 𝑖, we draw 𝑘 = 16 independent baseline samples (𝑟1, . . . , 𝑟16), sort them by completion length, and uniformly partition them into 8 length buckets (2 samples per bucket), denoted 𝑎𝑖,1 (very short) through 𝑎𝑖,8 (very long). Aggregation across problems then averages within each bucket index, producing the 8 data points per (model, method) curve in Figure 4. Critically, the binning is done per problem: each 𝑎𝑖,𝑘 refers to problem 𝑖’s own 𝑘-th length level relative to its own sampling distribution, not to a global token threshold. This controls for variation in problem difficulty.

Figure 8 | Two-step training construction for the alternative multi-round method. Step 1 applies a round-specific visibility mask over the concatenated trajectory; Step 2 removes masked tokens round by round and bundles the resulting traces so that training-time attention and position indices match rollout.

Figure 1 | Accuracy vs. mean tokens for Baseline (standard CoT) and InsightReplay (1, 3, 5 replay rounds), averaged over three 30B-tier models on the LiveCodeBench v5 subset. Each star reports the overall mean accuracy.
Figure 9 | Validation accuracy on AIME 2025 for the alternative training method over a unified 420-step budget. We report best@32, maj@32, and mean@32 throughout training.

Figure 5 | Validation accuracy on AIME 2025 throughout GRPO training, under InsightReplay rollouts vs. standard CoT rollouts. Across all three metrics (best@32, maj@32, mean@32). Dashed curves show cubic polynomial trend lines; the fitting procedure is described in Appendix G.
Table 1 | Effect of thinking content on answer probability 𝑃(ans) on 60 AIME problems with Qwen3-8B. ✓ = present, – = absent, ∅ = random tokens length-matched to CoT reasoning trace.
Table 4 | Full RL training hyperparameters. Values are shared by the baseline and InsightReplay runs unless otherwise noted.

Table 2 | Main inference results on four reasoning benchmarks. Base: standard thinking-mode CoT inference. VO (Verify-Only): a control for test-time scaling, in which the model is instructed to keep thinking after its initial trace but performs no insight extraction or replay. IR1/IR3: InsightReplay with 1 or 3 rounds of extract-then-replay. Accuracy gains over Base are highlighted in blue when positive and red when negative; token columns report average completion tokens per sample.

Figure 2 | Average pre-softmax attention from the answer token to insights vs. insertion ratio 𝜌 at each model’s final attention layer, under native scaling (q · k/√𝑑head for Qwen3-8B; q · k for Gemma-4-31B-it). Annotations show relative decline from 𝜌=0− 0.4 (paired bootstrap, 𝑝 < 0.001).

Figure 7 | Length-sweep results for ∼ 8B-scale models (Qwen3.5-9B, Gemma-4-E4B, and R1-Distill-Qwen-7B), averaged with equal weight over the four benchmarks. Dashed lines mark each variant’s peak (mean of its top-3 length levels). The same rightward and upward shift of the IR3 curve relative to Base observed at the 30B scale (Figure 4) holds at the 8B scale, confirming that the inverted-U reshaping is consistent across model sizes.

Figure 4 | Accuracy vs. mean completion tokens for three ∼ 30B-scale models, averaged over the four benchmarks. The Baseline curve traces accuracy across 8 length levels of standard CoT; each star marker reports the peak accuracy of VO, IR1, or IR3 on the same set of problems. Dashed lines connect the Baseline peak to the IR1 and IR3 peaks, visualizing how peak accuracy evolves with the number of replay rounds. Results for ∼ 8B-scale models are shown in Appendix F due to space.
Figure 3 | Illustration of InsightReplay. : input question, : reasoning chunk, : insight, : final
